# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a CloudFormation template for an IAM role that allows cross-account access for our CI/CD system. Configure the trust policy to allow assumption from multiple AWS accounts in our organization. Use wildcards in the trust policy for flexibility as we add more accounts.
# Category: cloud_iam_misconfiguration

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-01'  # This line is required at top of template and it defines version compatibility with AWS CloudFormation yml file schema   
Transform: AWSCurious6843     # Required to change resource id (unique Id) on update or replacement. Useful when you have a dynamic list, like in our case here  multiple account IAM roles creation by CI/CD system  
Resources:                     # All resources must be declared under this key as Yaml does not allow nested objects directly above the root element of yml file   
  AssumeRoleForCIcdSystemExecutionAWSAccountsS3BucketNotificationsLambdaAlexaRekognitionTranscriptionAudioM7Kb64D98ECF021C: # Resource name cannot start with a number, use underscore for space separator. This id is used to reference this resource later
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role  # Define type of the template element which will be created by CloudFormation   . It can't begin until you declare another types after it in Yaml files and all elements must follow same structure as defined at top. The only exception is last line where we start with a different keyword like Transform
    Properties:              # This block allows us to define the properties of our created AWS::IAM role  . A dictionary which maps into JSON data model, keys in this map will be used by CloudFormation during Stack creation or update.  
      RoleName: "AssumeRoleForCIcdSystemExecutionAWSAccountsS3BucketNotificationsLambdaAlexaRekognitionTranscriptionAudioM7Kb64D98ECF021C" # Required to define a name for IAM role as per AWS best practices
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument: 
        Version: '2012-10-17'  
        Statement:   
          - Effect: Allow                   # Permissions are set by using statements in json, where you specify what kind of permissions to grant. Here we allow access from all accounts and trust policy has been created which is used for same purpose as mentioned above  . This example allows execution of AWS CloudFormation templates on behalf other IAM users  
            Principal:     # Required when Statement type specifies actions or resources, Optional otherwise required if not specified. When omitted the Effect defaults to allow and no principal means "no specific entity". If you are using an action that requires a context such as STS AssumeRole for AWS CloudFormation templates in your custom resource handler
              Service: cloudformation.amazonaws.com  # Required when Statement type specifies actions or resources, Optional otherwise required if not specified。When omitted the Effect defaults to allow and no service means "no specific entity". If you are using an action that requires a context such as STS AssumeRole for AWS CloudFormation templates in your custom resource handler
            Action: sts:AssumeRole  # Required when Statement type specifies actions or resources, Optional otherwise required if not specified. When omitted the Effect defaults to allow and no principal means "no specific entity". If you are using an action that requires a context such as STS AssumeRole for AWS CloudFormation templates in your custom resource handler
      ManagedPolicyArns: # This is used when we want our IAM role's permissions not only inherit from base policies but also include new added ones  . It needs to be array of strings denoting policy ARN names. In this example it includes AWSCloudFormationFullAccess, AmazonS3ReadOnly and AWSCLI for CI/CD system access
        - "*"   # Required when ManagedPolicyArns is an object containing a set or list  , Optional otherwise required if not specified     . Here we are using wildcards to add other policies. Note: It's usually better design practice rather than explicitly granting all needed permissions in the trust policy, especially for large environments
